explanation: SET NOCOUNT is explicitly turned OFF, leaving row-count messages enabled which adds network chatter for each statement.
SET XACT_ABORT is OFF while the procedure uses explicit transactions, so some run-time errors can leave a transaction partially applied.
SELECT * on Sales.Orders couples callers to every current and future column and returns large LOB comment columns needlessly.
SELECT TOP (10) from Sales.Orders has no ORDER BY, so the ten returned rows are arbitrary and non-deterministic.
DISTINCT on o.OrderID is redundant because OrderID is the primary key and already unique.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders permits dirty reads that can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) wraps the OrderDate column in a function, preventing an index range seek; use a date range comparison instead.
Comparing int column o.CustomerID with nvarchar variable @CustomerIdText forces an implicit conversion that can defeat index usage.
The @CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID pattern makes cardinality estimation poor and typically forces a scan.
LIKE N'%urgent%' begins with a wildcard on o.Comments, preventing any index seek.
NOT IN over Sales.Invoices.CustomerID can yield no rows if any CustomerID is NULL; use NOT EXISTS instead.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) = 1 wraps the column in a function, making the predicate non-sargable.
o.OrderID + 0 = @OrderID performs arithmetic on the indexed key, preventing a direct index seek.
o.CustomerID <> 1 is a negative predicate that typically scans most of the table.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers has no join predicate and multiplies the row counts.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row from the cross join.
Comma-separated FROM list with the join condition in WHERE is legacy join syntax; use explicit INNER JOIN.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
The correlated (SELECT COUNT(*) ... WHERE ol.OrderID = o.OrderID) runs a scalar lookup per outer row; a GROUP BY join is more efficient.
COUNT(*) is computed as LineCount but only the number is used; if existence were needed EXISTS would be cheaper.
The correlated EXISTS against Sales.OrderLines runs per outer row and could be rewritten as a semi-join.
The CASE WHEN o.Comments IS NULL expression is repeated in both the select list and ORDER BY; compute it once via CROSS APPLY or a CTE.
ORDER BY 2 relies on select-list position and breaks when the column order changes; order by the column name.
OFFSET/FETCH orders only by o.OrderDate, which is not unique, so pagination results are unstable without a tie-breaker.
The two branches filter on OrderID = 1 and OrderID = 2, which are disjoint, so UNION performs a needless distinct sort; use UNION ALL.
(SELECT COUNT(*) ...) > 0 counts all matching order lines when EXISTS would short-circuit at the first match.
The correlated scalar COUNT subquery is evaluated per outer row of Sales.Orders.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID from a multi-row source silently keeps only the last value with no ordering guarantee.
The table variable @OrderIds may receive a fixed low cardinality estimate, leading to poor plans when populated with non-trivial data.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders builds the temp table schema implicitly; declare it explicitly for stable typing.
SELECT TOP (5) INTO without ORDER BY captures an arbitrary set of rows.
A cursor over Sales.Orders processes rows one at a time; this work can be done set-based.
SELECT TOP (2) in the cursor declaration lacks ORDER BY, so the two rows are arbitrary.
A WHILE loop iterates instead of performing a set-based operation.
SQL text is built by concatenating @CustomerID into the string, risking plan bloat and injection.
EXEC (@Sql) runs concatenated SQL without parameterization.
The @OrderID value is concatenated into the SQL string instead of being bound as a parameter.
sp_executesql is called with an embedded value rather than a declared @param binding.
#Scratch is created but never dropped before the procedure ends.
This BEGIN TRY covers a divide operation but note the broader pattern; the CATCH below swallows the error.
The CATCH block only selects ERROR_MESSAGE() and neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status, hiding the failure from callers.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path; prefer THROW for consistent error behavior.
PRINT is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics.
o.CustomerID = 1 embeds a business identifier directly rather than accepting it as a parameter.
The unlabelled date constant '20160101' is embedded in query logic without a named parameter or comment.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') has no style parameter, so the parsed value depends on session date format settings.
COALESCE on both sides of the join predicate prevents normal join optimization and index seeks.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary joined row.
The self-join of Sales.Orders on OrderID only returns the same key already present in the outer row.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts by an nvarchar(MAX) LOB column, which is expensive and unselective.
SUM(ol.Quantity) aggregates the entire Sales.OrderLines fact table with no restrictive WHERE clause.
HAVING ol.OrderID > 0 filters individual rows and should be in the WHERE clause before grouping.
ORDER BY on a COUNT(*) aggregate without GROUP BY is redundant since the query returns a single row.
MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; use separate IF EXISTS / UPDATE / INSERT blocks.
UPDATE SET target.Comments = target.Comments in the MERGE changes no data yet still logs and locks.
UPDATE Sales.Orders SET Comments = Comments writes the same value, causing needless logging and locking.
DELETE FROM Sales.OrderLines WHERE Quantity = -1 targets rows by a broad non-key value rather than a stable key.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a worker thread and holds resources instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION here is not wrapped in a TRY/CATCH boundary, so an error can leave the transaction open.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT Sales.Orders ON changes session state and introduces fragile identity-management coupling.
EXEC sp_who calls a system routine without schema qualification; unqualified sp_ names also trigger extra resolution lookups.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes session state and is never restored, silently limiting subsequent statements.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES (99, @NewCategory) has no explicit column list, making it fragile to schema changes.
The literal 99 is a magic category identifier embedded directly in the INSERT.
WHERE CustomerName LIKE '%' + @CustomerName + '%' has a leading wildcard, preventing an index seek on CustomerName.
The @CustomerName value is concatenated into the dynamic SQL string instead of being passed as a parameter.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into the executed SQL string, exposing a SQL injection vulnerability.
sp_executesql is invoked with a fully concatenated string and no parameter bindings.
sp_executesql is called without the sys schema qualifier.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers SET CustomerCategoryID = 1 has no WHERE clause and updates every row in the table.
The WHERE o.OrderDate >= '2016-01-01' predicate on the LEFT-joined Sales.Orders removes NULL rows, effectively turning the outer join into an inner join.
The unlabelled date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded directly in the filter logic.
WHERE DeliveryMethodID = NULL never matches any row; use IS NULL instead.
dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) is a scalar UDF invoked per row in the select list, forcing row-by-row evaluation.
The CATCH block runs inside an explicit transaction but does not ROLLBACK, leaving the transaction open on error.
The CATCH only selects ERROR_MESSAGE() and neither rethrows nor returns a failure status.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hardcodes an index hint on Sales.Customers, which can break if indexing changes.
WHERE CustomerName LIKE @CustomerName may carry a leading wildcard value, preventing an index seek.
The query nests derived tables three levels deep (t1/t2/t3); refactor to a CTE or single query for readability and optimization.
LIKE '%A%' begins with a wildcard, preventing an index seek on CustomerName.
SELECT * from the table-valued function couples callers to its full output shape.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti appears to be a multi-statement TVF whose fixed row estimate hurts plans; an inline TVF would optimize better.
